learning_objectives: Define health informatics and its scope
Identify key domains within the field
Understand the role of informatics in modern healthcare
readings: Health Informatics: An Interprofessional Approach Ch. 1
AMIA White Paper on Health Informatics Workforce

lecture_notes.introduction: Health informatics sits at the intersection of information science, computer science, and healthcare. This course introduces you to how data and technology transform patient care and health systems.
key_concepts: Definition and scope of health informatics
Biomedical informatics vs. health informatics vs. clinical informatics
The informatics workforce and career paths
Historical evolution from paper records to digital health
Core competencies for health informatics professionals
lecture_notes.demonstration: Overview of real-world health informatics systems including EHR dashboards and public health surveillance tools

review.value: The interdisciplinary study of the design, development, adoption, and application of IT-based innovations in healthcare
Clinical informatics, public health informatics, consumer health informatics, bioinformatics

class_schedule.topic: History and Evolution of Health IT
learning_objectives: Trace the historical development of health information technology
Identify major legislative milestones such as HITECH Act
Understand how policy drives technology adoption in healthcare
readings: Health Informatics: An Interprofessional Approach Ch. 2

lecture_notes.introduction: Understanding where health IT has been helps us navigate where it is going. We trace the arc from early hospital information systems through meaningful use.
key_concepts: Early hospital information systems of the 1960s-70s
Rise of clinical decision support in the 1980s-90s
HIPAA (1996) and its informatics implications
HITECH Act (2009) and Meaningful Use
21st Century Cures Act and information blocking
lecture_notes.demonstration: Timeline walkthrough of major health IT milestones with screenshots of legacy and modern systems
setup_requirements: Access to ONC Health IT timeline resource

review.value: Health Information Technology for Economic and Clinical Health Act (2009) provided $27 billion in incentives for EHR adoption
CMS program requiring providers to demonstrate they use EHRs in ways that improve care quality

learning_objectives: Classify different types of healthcare data
Explain the role of data standards in interoperability
Identify major health data standards including HL7 and FHIR
readings: Health Informatics: An Interprofessional Approach Ch. 5

lecture_notes.introduction: Healthcare generates enormous volumes of structured and unstructured data. Standards are the glue that allows these data to flow between systems.
key_concepts: Structured vs. unstructured clinical data
HL7 v2 messaging standard
HL7 FHIR (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources)
Terminology standards: ICD-10, SNOMED CT, LOINC, CPT
The interoperability challenge in healthcare
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walkthrough of an HL7 FHIR Patient resource in JSON format and comparison with HL7 v2 ADT message
setup_requirements: Web browser for FHIR sandbox exploration

review.value: Modern RESTful API standard for exchanging healthcare data using JSON/XML resources
International Classification of Diseases used for diagnosis coding with over 70,000 codes

learning_objectives: Describe common architectures for health information systems
Differentiate between client-server, cloud, and hybrid models
Understand integration engines and middleware in healthcare
readings: Health Informatics: An Interprofessional Approach Ch. 6

lecture_notes.introduction: Modern healthcare relies on interconnected systems. Understanding how these systems are architected is fundamental to the informatics professional.
key_concepts: Monolithic vs. distributed system architectures
Integration engines (Mirth Connect, Rhapsody)
Cloud computing models in healthcare (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS)
Service-oriented architecture and microservices
API-driven healthcare system design
lecture_notes.demonstration: Diagram walkthrough of a typical hospital IT architecture showing EHR, PACS, LIS, pharmacy, and integration layer

review.value: Middleware that routes, transforms, and manages messages between disparate healthcare systems
Cloud-hosted applications like athenahealth or Cerner CommunityWorks reducing on-premises infrastructure

learning_objectives: Explain the HIPAA Privacy and Security Rules
Understand the role of the informatics professional in compliance
readings: Health Informatics: An Interprofessional Approach Ch. 10
HHS HIPAA Summary of the Privacy Rule

lecture_notes.introduction: Protecting patient data is both a legal obligation and an ethical imperative. Every informatics professional must understand HIPAA inside and out.
key_concepts: HIPAA Privacy Rule: permitted uses and disclosures
HIPAA Security Rule: administrative, physical, and technical safeguards
18 PHI identifiers and de-identification methods
Breach notification requirements
Business Associate Agreements (BAAs)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study analysis of the Anthem data breach (2015) examining root causes and HIPAA violations
setup_requirements: HHS breach portal access for case studies

review.value: Protected Health Information includes any individually identifiable health information held by a covered entity
Administrative (policies), Physical (facility access), Technical (encryption, access control)

class_schedule.topic: Emerging Trends: AI, Telehealth, and the Future
learning_objectives: Identify emerging technologies reshaping health informatics
Evaluate the impact of AI and machine learning on clinical workflows
Discuss ethical considerations of emerging health technologies
readings: Health Informatics: An Interprofessional Approach Ch. 20
WHO Guidance on Ethics and Governance of AI for Health (2021)

lecture_notes.introduction: The field of health informatics is evolving rapidly. This session examines the technologies that will define the next decade of healthcare delivery.
key_concepts: Artificial intelligence and machine learning in diagnostics
Natural language processing for clinical documentation
Telehealth expansion post-COVID-19
Wearable devices and remote patient monitoring
Blockchain applications in health data management
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live demo of an FDA-approved AI diagnostic tool and comparison with clinician interpretation
setup_requirements: Internet access for live demo viewing

review.value: AI systems that assist clinicians in diagnosis, treatment planning, or risk prediction using patient data
Use of connected devices to collect and transmit patient health data outside traditional care settings

questions.question: Explain the difference between health informatics, clinical informatics, and bioinformatics. Provide one example use case for each.
questions.answer: Health informatics is the broad field studying IT applications in healthcare. Clinical informatics focuses on optimizing clinical workflows and EHR use at the point of care. Bioinformatics applies computational methods to biological data such as genomic sequences. Example: Health informatics - designing a public health surveillance dashboard; Clinical informatics - implementing CPOE in a hospital; Bioinformatics - analyzing gene expression data for cancer research.

rubric.key: Correct definitions of all three domains

questions.question: What was the HITECH Act and how did it accelerate EHR adoption in the United States?
questions.answer: The HITECH Act (2009) was part of the American Recovery and Reinvestment Act. It provided approximately $27 billion in incentive payments through Medicare and Medicaid to eligible providers and hospitals that demonstrated Meaningful Use of certified EHR technology. It also established penalties for non-adoption and strengthened HIPAA enforcement. By 2017, over 95% of hospitals had adopted certified EHRs, up from about 12% in 2009.

rubric.key: Identifies HITECH as part of ARRA 2009
Explains financial incentives and Meaningful Use
Mentions measurable impact on adoption rates

questions.question: Compare HL7 v2 and HL7 FHIR. Why is the industry moving toward FHIR for healthcare data exchange?
questions.answer: HL7 v2 uses pipe-delimited message segments and has been the workhorse of healthcare interoperability since the 1990s but is difficult to implement consistently due to optionality and local variations. FHIR uses modern web standards (RESTful APIs, JSON/XML) with well-defined resources, making it easier for developers to build interoperable applications. The industry favors FHIR because it reduces implementation complexity, supports mobile and web apps, has strong community tooling, and is mandated by the 21st Century Cures Act for patient access APIs.

rubric.key: Accurate description of HL7 v2 characteristics
Reasons for industry shift including regulatory drivers

questions.question: List and explain the three categories of safeguards required by the HIPAA Security Rule. Give one specific example of each.
questions.answer: Administrative safeguards: policies and procedures to manage the selection, development, and maintenance of security measures (e.g., workforce training programs on PHI handling). Physical safeguards: physical measures to protect electronic systems and buildings from natural hazards and unauthorized intrusion (e.g., badge-controlled access to server rooms). Technical safeguards: technology and related policies to protect ePHI and control access (e.g., AES-256 encryption of data at rest and in transit).

rubric.key: Correct identification and explanation of all three categories
Valid specific example for each category
Demonstrates understanding of how safeguards protect ePHI

major_assignments.title: Health Informatics Landscape Analysis
major_assignments.description: Research and present a comprehensive analysis of a specific health informatics domain (e.g., clinical informatics, public health informatics, consumer health informatics). Include current state, key technologies, challenges, and future outlook.
objectives: Demonstrate understanding of a specific health informatics subdomain
Analyze challenges and barriers to adoption
deliverables: Annotated bibliography of at least 10 peer-reviewed sources

criteria: Use of current and authoritative sources
Clear articulation of future directions and implications

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of core health informatics concepts and terminology

class_participation.description: Active engagement in discussions, case studies, and in-class activities

major_assignments.description: Research paper and presentation on a health informatics domain

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive assessment covering foundational concepts and standards

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all course material including emerging trends

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance is expected. Students missing more than 3 classes without documented excuse may have their final grade reduced by one letter.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments submitted late will lose 10% per day up to 5 days. After 5 days, assignments receive a zero unless prior arrangements are made with the instructor.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of concepts is encouraged, but all written work must be individually produced. Group projects require equal contribution documented via peer evaluations.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Plagiarism, including uncited use of AI-generated text, will result in a zero on the assignment and possible course failure. All sources must be properly cited in APA format.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a laptop with internet access for in-class exercises. Access to the university LMS and the FHIR sandbox environment will be provided.
